title: Why it's called "Lavender"
moment: Newly made a coach, Nate snaps at Will, the young kit-man who took over his old laundry job, for switching to lavender-scented fabric softener -- a tiny change he can't abide because he can't yet abide no longer being the kit-man.
idea: The whole episode is about people clinging to who they used to be -- Nate to his old job, Roy to playing, Jamie to his stardom -- and the title quietly hangs that theme on one small smell.
why: A perfect 'the title is a clue' card: the pettiest detail in the episode is also its thesis. Notice how Nate's cruelty to Will is really fear about his own new, unfamiliar self.
source: Recaps in The Spool, Music City Drive-In, and NPR's 'The Prodigal Beefcake'; Ted Lasso Fandom 'Lavender' entry.
groundingNotes: Lavender-fabric-softener / Nate-Will origin of the title confirmed across multiple independent recaps and Fandom; grounding capped at 4 (no primary page openable this session).

title: "Lust Conquers All"
moment: The episode opens inside a fictional dating-competition show, 'Lust Conquers All' -- villa, confessionals, eliminations -- where Jamie, cast as the resident bad boy, is unexpectedly voted off after a run of romantic flip-flops.
idea: It's a precise parody of ITV's reality juggernaut Love Island, and it reframes Jamie's whole arc: he chased fame instead of football, and it collapsed on him on national TV.
why: The spoof is the setup for the episode's real question -- whether a humbled Jamie deserves a way back. Watch how the show uses a silly genre to puncture his ego before it lets him ask for help.
source: Ted Lasso Fandom 'Lust Conquers All' entry; thedipp and NPR recaps confirming the Love Island parody.
groundingNotes: Cold-open 'Lust Conquers All' as a Love Island parody with Jamie eliminated confirmed across Fandom, thedipp, NPR, Elite Daily; grounding capped at 4.

title: The prodigal son comes home
moment: Humiliated, released by Manchester City, and wanted by no club after his reality-TV detour, Jamie quietly finds Ted at a bar and asks to come back to Richmond. Ted's first instinct is no, and the Diamond Dogs are split.
idea: Critics read the arc as a prodigal-son story (NPR literally titled its recap 'The Prodigal Beefcake'): the show's recurring question of whether people get a second chance, and on what terms. The philosophical hook is friendship and forgiveness -- not whether Jamie is useful, but whether he's wanted.
why: Hold this up against the episode's other can't-let-go-of-the-past stories. The 'prodigal son' is a reading, not the writers' label -- but it names exactly what's at stake when a humbled man asks to come home.
source: NPR's 'The Prodigal Beefcake' recap and AwardsWatch; Salon on Jamie's redemption arc; plot via the Wikipedia episode entry.
groundingNotes: Plot (Jamie unwanted, asks Ted to return, Dogs split) and the widely-shared prodigal-son reading confirmed across NPR, AwardsWatch, Salon, Wikipedia; framed as a reading, not creator intent. Grounding capped at 4.

tag: A pun hidden in a houseplant
title: The Led Zeppelin gag
moment: Passing the displaced Higgins -- who keeps getting moved between temporary offices -- Ted says hello to an office plant, calling it 'Robert.' Higgins, mid-move with a fistful of papers, deadpans that his name is 'Jimmy.'
idea: It's a blink-and-miss double pun on Led Zeppelin: Robert Plant (the potted plant) and Jimmy Page (the sheaf of pages Higgins is holding).
why: Pure Easter egg -- the kind of throwaway wordplay the show layers onto a single prop. Once you see it you'll never un-see it, and it rewards a pause on the rewatch.
source: Episode IMDb trivia, the Nerds & Beyond recap, and the lassoism S2E2 transcript, all reading it as Plant/Page.
groundingNotes: 'Robert' plant / 'Jimmy' papers Led Zeppelin pun confirmed across IMDb trivia, a recap, and a transcript; grounding capped at 4 (no primary page openable).

title: Punditry with real faces
moment: Pushed by Keeley, a restless retired Roy reluctantly takes a punditry gig on Sky Sports' Soccer Saturday -- sharing the desk with real broadcasters Jeff Stelling and Chris Kamara (as themselves) and the fictional, Ted-hating George Cartrick. Asked about his old club, Roy swears about Chelsea on live TV.
idea: Another real-media cameo that grounds Richmond in actual British football culture -- and a perfectly in-character beat: Roy is incapable of pretending, even on air, even about the club he loved.
why: Watch Stelling have to apologise to viewers. The bluntness that makes Roy a liability on a polite TV panel is the same honesty that will make him a great coach.
source: Ted Lasso Fandom pages for Jeff Stelling, Chris Kamara, and George Cartrick; IMDb episode cast listing.
groundingNotes: Roy's Soccer Saturday debut with real pundits Stelling and Kamara (plus fictional Cartrick) and the on-air Chelsea swearing confirmed across Fandom and IMDb; grounding capped at 4.
